exercises.title: Every tone points two ways
exercises.instructions: For each given tone, write the tone it generates (its strongest overtone, a fifth above) and the tone that generates it (its origin, a fifth below).

exercises.title: Every tone points two ways
exercises.instructions: For each given tone, write the tone it generates (its strongest overtone, a fifth above) and the tone that generates it (its origin, a fifth below).

exercises.title: Spell the overtone series
exercises.instructions: For each fundamental, write the first eight partials. The seventh partial is notated as a flat seventh — remember it is only an approximation of the real overtone.

exercises.title: Spell the overtone series
exercises.instructions: For each fundamental, write the first eight partials. The seventh partial is notated as a flat seventh — remember it is only an approximation of the real overtone.

exercises.title: Spell the diatonic triads in C major
exercises.instructions: For each scale degree shown, write the triad in root position (root, third, fifth).

exercises.title: Connect the triads in C major
exercises.instructions: Continue in four parts from the given chord. Root position, root doubled. Hold the common tone in the same voice (harmonisches Band); move every other voice the shortest way. No parallel fifths or octaves.

exercises.title: Connect the triads in C major

exercises.title: Little phrase (Sätzchen) in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize the degree plan in four parts, starting from the given chord. Root position, root doubled. Hold the common tone in the same voice; move every other voice the shortest way. The phrase begins and ends on I.

exercises.title: Little phrase (Sätzchen) in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize the degree plan in four parts, starting from the given chord. Root position, root doubled. Hold the common tone in the same voice; move every other voice the shortest way. The phrase begins and ends on I.

exercises.title: Little phrase (Sätzchen) in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize the degree plan in four parts, starting from the given chord. Root position, root doubled. Hold the common tone in the same voice; move every other voice the shortest way. The phrase begins and ends on I.

exercises.title: The seventh degree in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase in four parts from the given chord. In VII the diminished fifth must already stand in the same voice in the chord before (preparation), must fall by step into the next chord (resolution), and is never doubled. Double the third of VII, not its root.

exercises.title: The seventh degree in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase in four parts from the given chord. In VII the diminished fifth must already stand in the same voice in the chord before (preparation), must fall by step into the next chord (resolution), and is never doubled. Double the third of VII, not its root.

exercises.title: The cadential six-four in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase in four parts. The six-four chord doubles its bass tone, its bass is approached by common tone or step, and the next chord keeps the same bass: I 6/4 goes to V.

exercises.title: The cadential six-four in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase in four parts. The six-four chord doubles its bass tone, its bass is approached by common tone or step, and the next chord keeps the same bass: I 6/4 goes to V.

exercises.title: Sixth chords in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize the plan in four parts. Chords marked 6 take their third in the bass; do not double the bass tone of a sixth chord — double the root (or the fifth). All connection rules still hold.

exercises.title: Sixth chords in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize the plan in four parts. Chords marked 6 take their third in the bass; do not double the bass tone of a sixth chord — double the root (or the fifth). All connection rules still hold.

exercises.title: Sixth chords in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize the plan in four parts. Chords marked 6 take their third in the bass; do not double the bass tone of a sixth chord — double the root (or the fifth). All connection rules still hold.

exercises.title: Chains of seventh chords in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. In a chain of sevenths the falling seventh of one chord becomes preparation for the next; where the voices would deadlock, omit the fifth and double the root.

exercises.title: Chains of seventh chords in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. In a chain of sevenths the falling seventh of one chord becomes preparation for the next; where the voices would deadlock, omit the fifth and double the root.

exercises.title: Inversions of the seventh chord in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. The seventh keeps its duties in every position; in the 2-chord it lies in the bass, so the BASS falls by step and the next chord is a sixth chord.

exercises.title: Inversions of the seventh chord in C major

exercises.title: Seventh chords in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase in four parts from the given chord. The seventh must already stand in the same voice in the chord before (preparation) and falls by step in the chord after (resolution); the root moves a fourth upward.

exercises.title: Connections without a common tone in A minor
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. Where two chords share no tone (roots a second apart), every upper voice moves in contrary motion to the bass, each to the nearest chord tone. In minor, mind the turning points — the leading tone may rise with the bass, and the deceptive V-VI doubles the third of VI.

exercises.title: Design your own phrase in A minor
exercises.instructions: Design a degree plan of 8 chords and realize it in four parts. Rules of the game: begin and end on I; close with IV-I or V-I; use mostly ascending progressions (a fourth up or a third down); use at least one step progression; never two descending progressions in a row. The answer key shows ONE model solution — yours may differ and still be right.

exercises.title: Cadences in A minor
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase; its close is named in the exercise data. Authentic: V-I, approached through IV or ii. Half: the phrase rests on V — a comma, not a period. Plagal: IV-I after the authentic close. Full close: the cadential six-four announces V.

exercises.title: Cadences in A minor
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase; its close is named in the exercise data. Authentic: V-I, approached through IV or ii. Half: the phrase rests on V — a comma, not a period. Plagal: IV-I after the authentic close. Full close: the cadential six-four announces V.

exercises.title: The freer seventh degree in A minor
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. In first inversion, vii° needs no preparation: it passes between I and I6 (the bass walks by step), doubling its third. In root position, keep the old care.

exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. In first inversion, vii° needs no preparation: it passes between I and I6 (the bass walks by step), doubling its third. In root position, keep the old care.

exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. In first inversion, vii° needs no preparation: it passes between I and I6 (the bass walks by step), doubling its third. In root position, keep the old care.

exercises.title: Modulation from C major to F major
exercises.instructions: Realize each modulation in three phases: neutral chords that belong to both keys; the modulatory means — the first chord foreign to the old key; then a full cadence in the new key. The pivot chord is named in both keys.

exercises.title: Modulation from C major to F major
exercises.instructions: Realize each modulation in three phases: neutral chords that belong to both keys; the modulatory means — the first chord foreign to the old key; then a full cadence in the new key. The pivot chord is named in both keys.

exercises.title: Modulation from A minor to C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each modulation in three phases: neutral chords that belong to both keys; the modulatory means — the first chord foreign to the old key; then a full cadence in the new key. The pivot chord is named in both keys.

exercises.title: The diminished seventh in A minor
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. The diminished seventh chord needs no preparation — it is at home nowhere and welcome everywhere — but its duties on leaving remain: the seventh falls by step, the leading tone rises, and no tone is doubled.

exercises.title: The diminished seventh in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. The diminished seventh chord needs no preparation — it is at home nowhere and welcome everywhere — but its duties on leaving remain: the seventh falls by step, the leading tone rises, and no tone is doubled.

exercises.title: The diminished seventh in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. The diminished seventh chord needs no preparation — it is at home nowhere and welcome everywhere — but its duties on leaving remain: the seventh falls by step, the leading tone rises, and no tone is doubled.

exercises.title: Secondary dominants in A minor
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. A secondary dominant stands wherever its target degree could stand, and resolves to it: the chromatic tone is an artificial leading tone and rises by semitone; a seventh falls as always. Use them sparingly — three or four in a phrase of ten to fifteen chords.

exercises.title: Secondary dominants in A minor
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. A secondary dominant stands wherever its target degree could stand, and resolves to it: the chromatic tone is an artificial leading tone and rises by semitone; a seventh falls as always. Use them sparingly — three or four in a phrase of ten to fifteen chords.

exercises.title: Far modulations from C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each modulation. For the third and fourth circles the means is a single strong foreign chord — the dominant seventh (or diminished seventh) of the goal key — followed by a full cadence there. The new accidentals arrive in the means and are confirmed by the cadence.

exercises.title: Far modulations from C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each modulation. For the third and fourth circles the means is a single strong foreign chord — the dominant seventh (or diminished seventh) of the goal key — followed by a full cadence there. The new accidentals arrive in the means and are confirmed by the cadence.

exercises.title: The minor subdominant in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. The chords marked with lowered tones are borrowed from the parallel minor: the lowered sixth degree keeps its minor tendency and falls. IV followed by iv darkens the same function chromatically.

exercises.title: The minor subdominant in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. The chords marked with lowered tones are borrowed from the parallel minor: the lowered sixth degree keeps its minor tendency and falls. IV followed by iv darkens the same function chromatically.

exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. The chords marked with lowered tones are borrowed from the parallel minor: the lowered sixth degree keeps its minor tendency and falls. IV followed by iv darkens the same function chromatically.

exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. The chords marked with lowered tones are borrowed from the parallel minor: the lowered sixth degree keeps its minor tendency and falls. IV followed by iv darkens the same function chromatically.

exercises.title: The Neapolitan sixth in A minor
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. The Neapolitan sixth stands for II in the cadence: its bass tone is doubled, it leans on I6/4 or V, and the lowered second falls — to the tonic, or through the diminished third to the leading tone.

exercises.title: The Neapolitan sixth in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. The Neapolitan sixth stands for II in the cadence: its bass tone is doubled, it leans on I6/4 or V, and the lowered second falls — to the tonic, or through the diminished third to the leading tone.

exercises.title: The Neapolitan sixth in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. The Neapolitan sixth stands for II in the cadence: its bass tone is doubled, it leans on I6/4 or V, and the lowered second falls — to the tonic, or through the diminished third to the leading tone.

exercises.title: Augmented sixth chords in A minor
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. In every augmented sixth chord the bass (the lowered sixth degree) falls by semitone and the raised fourth rises by semitone — the interval stretches outward into the octave on the dominant. The 6/5 form passes through the six-four to avoid open fifths; the others may go straight to V.

exercises.title: Augmented sixth chords in C major
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. In every augmented sixth chord the bass (the lowered sixth degree) falls by semitone and the raised fourth rises by semitone — the interval stretches outward into the octave on the dominant. The 6/5 form passes through the six-four to avoid open fifths; the others may go straight to V.

exercises.title: The augmented triad in A minor
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. The augmented triad on III carries the raised seventh degree as its fifth: that tone is a turning point and rises. Do not double it.

exercises.title: The augmented triad in A minor
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. The augmented triad on III carries the raised seventh degree as its fifth: that tone is a turning point and rises. Do not double it.

exercises.title: The augmented triad in E minor
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase. The augmented triad on III carries the raised seventh degree as its fifth: that tone is a turning point and rises. Do not double it.

exercises.title: Long journeys from C major, by way of stations
exercises.instructions: Realize each journey. Divide the road: reach an intermediate key with its dominant seventh and touch its tonic — a station, not a home — then take the second leg the same way and settle the goal key with a full cadence. Beyond the sixth circle count the other way around: seven sharps is five flats.

exercises.title: Harmonize a chorale melody in A minor
exercises.instructions: The melody is given; write the three lower voices, one harmony per half note. Work out the close first, then from back to front. Guidelines: close on I; a melody tone is more often root or fifth than third; let no degree repeat itself needlessly; the answer is one model solution — yours may differ.

exercises.title: The dominant ninth in A minor
exercises.instructions: Spell the dominant ninth (fifth omitted in four voices) and resolve it like a seventh chord: the ninth and seventh fall by step, the third rises, the root leaps a fourth up. With the fifth kept, fifth and ninth would fall in parallel fifths — watch the fifths.

exercises.title: A suspension at the close in A minor
exercises.instructions: Realize each phrase; at the close, hold the V7's seventh over the tonic for half the measure, then resolve it down by step (4—3). The suspension is prepared (same voice in V7), and the resolution tone must not sound in another voice against it. The aim is to invent the delay with the harmony, not to decorate a finished phrase.

exercises.title: Altered dominants in A minor
exercises.instructions: Spell the altered dominant seventh and resolve it: the raised fifth rises by semitone (into a doubled third), the lowered fifth falls by semitone; the seventh falls, the third rises, the root leaps a fourth up. An altered tone is an artificial leading tone — it continues in the direction of its alteration.

exercises.title: The turning chord: one start, two roads (from C major)
exercises.instructions: Both phrases begin identically: I and its dominant seventh. Continue the first at home. In the second, the same sound is caught as the German sixth of the key a semitone below — the bass falls by semitone through the six-four onto the new dominant. Try the turning power of the chord, as the book asks.

exercises.title: Quartal chords from C major
exercises.instructions: Stack perfect fourths on the given tone — three to six of them, spelled exactly, fourth over fourth. Stack eleven and all twelve tones would sound.

exercises.title: Quartal chords from C major
exercises.instructions: Stack perfect fourths on the given tone — three to six of them, spelled exactly, fourth over fourth. Stack eleven and all twelve tones would sound.

exercises.title: Quartal chords from G major
exercises.instructions: Stack perfect fourths on the given tone — three to six of them, spelled exactly, fourth over fourth. Stack eleven and all twelve tones would sound.

exercises.title: The whole-tone dominant in A minor
exercises.instructions: Spell the whole-tone chord over the dominant root — all six tones of the scale — and resolve it strictly: the bass falls a fifth, lowered tones fall by semitone, raised tones rise, the ninth steps down; toward the minor tonic the raised fifth simply holds as the minor third.
